statement.en: Trustworthy Clinical Decision Support Using Meta-Predicates and Domain-Specific Languages
statement.summary:
\textbf{Background:} Regulatory frameworks for AI in healthcare, including the EU AI Act and FDA guidance on AI/ML-based medical devices, require clinical decision support to demonstrate not only accuracy but auditability. Existing formal languages for clinical logic validate syntactic and structural correctness but not whether decision rules use epistemologically appropriate evidence.
  \textbf{Methods:} Drawing on design-by-contract principles, we introduce meta-predicates -- predicates about predicates -- for asserting epistemological constraints on clinical decision rules expressed in a DSL. An epistemological type system classifies annotations along four dimensions: purpose, knowledge domain, scale, and method of acquisition. Meta-predicates assert which evidence types are permissible

proposer: Michael Bouzinier, Sergey Trifonov, Michael Chumack et al.

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: Paper proposes novel meta-predicate framework (2026 preprint, not yet peer-validated in clinical deployment). The core axiom—that epistemological type systems can enforce appropriate evidence hierarchies in clinical DSLs—is theoretically sound but empirically unvalidated at regulatory scale. Represents provisional contribution under active validation.

ramanujanFormula.interpretation: 述語の述語が、証拠の認識論的系統を保証する階層構造。
